title: Three Great Families of Faith
sub_domain_name: The Abrahamic Traditions: Judaism, Christianity & Islam

required.title: [Read Aloud] The Story of God
required.author: adapted selections or similar age-appropriate overview

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Stories from the Hebrew Bible / Torah

competence_markers.description: The student can tell the story of Abraham and explain why he matters to all three traditions
The student can describe the basic beliefs of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam in their own words
The student can name at least three things the traditions share and at least two things they disagree about
The student can describe how each tradition worships — sabbath, Sunday services, daily prayers
The student narrates each tradition's story with respect and accuracy, not caricature
The student shows curiosity about the differences rather than dismissing unfamiliar traditions
The student can identify moral teachings common to all three traditions

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of Abraham.
narration_prompts.detail: Why do Jews, Christians, and Muslims all consider him important?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick one of the three traditions and tell me what they believe.
narration_prompts.detail: What do they think about God? How do they worship? What do they think is most important?

narration_prompts.prompt: What do Judaism, Christianity, and Islam have in common?
narration_prompts.detail: Name at least three things they share.

narration_prompts.prompt: Where do the three traditions disagree?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick one major disagreement and explain what each side believes.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a sacred space
narration_prompts.detail: — a synagogue, a church, or a mosque. What does it look like? What happens there?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do you think people have fought over these religions?
narration_prompts.detail: If they worship the same God, why do they disagree so much?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the most interesting or surprising thing you learned about one of these traditions?

cross_connections.description: The ancient Near East where Judaism emerged; the Roman Empire where Christianity spread; the Arabian Peninsula where Islam began.
Each tradition answers the big questions differently. Apply the worldview framework.
The medieval world was shaped by the interaction and conflict of these three traditions.
The relationship between religious authority and law begins here — the Torah as law, the Quran as governance, Christendom as political order.

content.why_this_matters:
More than half the people alive on Earth today belong to one of three religions that all started with the same story: the story of a man named Abraham, who believed there was only one God.

From Abraham's story grew three great traditions — Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. They are called the Abrahamic traditions because they all trace themselves back to Abraham and his God. They share a common root, the way three branches of a tree share the same trunk. But the branches grew in very different directions, and the people who belong to these traditions have spent centuries agreeing about some things and fighting bitterly about others.

These three traditions have shaped more of the world you live in than any other force in history. The laws of your country, the calendar you use, the holidays you celebrate, the moral ideas you carry — even if you are not religious — were shaped by these traditions. Understanding them is not optional. It is the beginning of understanding the world.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The shared story** — Abraham, monotheism, and the common root of all three traditions
2. **The distinctive story of each tradition** — told with respect and from within its own logic
3. **The moral content** — what each tradition teaches about how to live, what is right and wrong, what God expects
4. **The sacred practices** — how each community worships, prays, and celebrates
5. **The common ground** — what all three share: one God, moral law, justice, mercy, the dignity of human beings
6. **The honest differences** — what they disagree about and why
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Abraham — Where the Story Begins

Thousands of years ago, in a land that is now Iraq, there lived a man named Abraham. According to the tradition, Abraham lived in a world where people worshipped many gods — gods of the sun, the moon, the rain, the harvest. But Abraham came to believe that there was only one God — one creator of everything, invisible and powerful and just.

God made a promise to Abraham: I will make you the father of a great nation. Your descendants will be as numerous as the stars. I will give them a land. And through you, all the families of the earth will be blessed.

This promise — this *covenant* — is the foundation of everything that follows. Judaism, Christianity, and Islam all claim Abraham as their ancestor. They all worship the one God Abraham believed in. They call themselves the children of Abraham. But they disagree — deeply and sometimes violently — about what God's covenant means, who it includes, and what God requires.

### Part 2: Judaism — The Covenant and the Law

Judaism is the oldest of the three traditions. The Jewish story goes like this:

God chose Abraham, and Abraham's descendants — the Israelites — became God's people. God gave them a law — the Torah — through Moses on Mount Sinai. The Torah contains 613 commandments, including the Ten Commandments: do not murder, do not steal, do not lie, honor your father and mother, have no other gods before me.

The covenant is a relationship: God promises to be their God; they promise to follow God's law. When they follow it, things go well. When they break it, there are consequences. The prophets — Isaiah, Jeremiah, Amos — are God's messengers who call the people back to the covenant when they stray.

**What makes Judaism special:** The Jewish tradition invented the idea that there is one God who cares about justice and mercy — not a distant, indifferent force but a God who has opinions about how you treat the poor, the widow, the orphan, and the stranger. This idea changed the world.

**The Sabbath:** Every week, Jews rest on the Sabbath — from Friday evening to Saturday evening. This is not just a day off. It is a commandment: God rested on the seventh day after creating the world, and so must you. And not just you — your servants, your animals, even the land. The Sabbath is one of the oldest human rights practices in history.

**The synagogue:** The Jewish house of worship. After the Temple in Jerusalem was destroyed (twice — in 586 BCE and 70 CE), Jews built synagogues wherever they went. The Torah is read aloud in the synagogue, and the community gathers to pray, study, and argue. Arguing about the law is a sacred practice in Judaism — the Talmud is a record of centuries of rabbis arguing about how to live well.

### Part 3: Christianity — The Story of Jesus

Christianity grows out of Judaism. The Christian story goes like this:

About 2,000 years ago, a Jewish man named Jesus was born in Bethlehem. Christians believe Jesus was not just a man but God himself, come to earth in human form — the Son of God.

Jesus taught about love, forgiveness, and the Kingdom of God. He said: love your neighbor as yourself. He said: love your enemies. He said: the last shall be first, and the first shall be last. He spent his time with the poor, the sick, and the outcasts — the people nobody else wanted to be around.

The religious authorities and the Roman government saw Jesus as a threat. He was arrested, tried, and crucified — nailed to a cross and left to die. Christians believe that three days after his death, Jesus rose from the dead — the Resurrection. They believe that through his death and resurrection, Jesus opened a way for all human beings to be forgiven and to have eternal life with God.

**What makes Christianity special:** The claim that God became a human being — that the creator of the universe chose to experience human suffering, hunger, loneliness, and death. In Christianity, God does not remain distant. God enters the story.

**The church:** Christians worship in churches. They gather on Sunday — the day of the Resurrection. They pray, sing, read from the Bible, and share communion — bread and wine that represent (or, in some traditions, become) the body and blood of Jesus.

**The Bible:** The Christian sacred book contains two parts: the Old Testament (which includes the Jewish Torah and other Hebrew scriptures) and the New Testament (which tells the story of Jesus and the early church). Christians and Jews share much of their scripture — but they read it very differently.

### Part 4: Islam — The Final Revelation

Islam is the youngest of the three traditions. The Islamic story goes like this:

In the year 610 CE, a man named Muhammad was meditating in a cave near the city of Mecca in Arabia. The angel Gabriel appeared to him and said: "Read!" Muhammad received a revelation from God — a message that would continue for the next twenty-three years and would become the Quran, the holy book of Islam.

Muslims believe the Quran is the direct word of God — not inspired by God, like Christians believe about the Bible, but literally the word of God, transmitted through Muhammad. They believe Muhammad is the last in a long line of prophets that includes Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. Muhammad is the Seal of the Prophets — the final messenger.

**What makes Islam special:** The absolute oneness of God — called Allah in Arabic. There is no God but God. This is the simplest, most powerful monotheistic claim in the Abrahamic tradition. Islam strips away everything between the human being and God — no priesthood, no hierarchy, no intermediaries. You stand before God directly.

**The Five Pillars:** Every Muslim's life is organized around five practices:
1. *Shahada* — the declaration: "There is no god but God, and Muhammad is his messenger."
2. *Salat* — five daily prayers, facing Mecca.
3. *Zakat* — giving a portion of your wealth to those in need.
4. *Sawm* — fasting during the month of Ramadan.
5. *Hajj* — the pilgrimage to Mecca, at least once in a lifetime if possible.

**The mosque:** Muslims worship in mosques. The call to prayer — the adhan — rings out five times a day. Muslims pray facing Mecca, the city where the Kaaba stands — the most sacred site in Islam, built (Muslims believe) by Abraham himself.

### Part 5: What They Share

These three traditions disagree about many things. But they also share a great deal:

- **One God** — the same God, the God of Abraham. They call God by different names and understand God differently, but they all worship the God who spoke to Abraham.
- **Moral law** — all three traditions teach that God cares about how you behave. Murder is wrong. Theft is wrong. Lying is wrong. You must care for the poor and the vulnerable.
- **Justice and mercy** — all three traditions wrestle with the relationship between justice (giving people what they deserve) and mercy (giving people better than they deserve). This tension runs through all three.
- **Sacred scripture** — all three are traditions of the Book. The Torah, the Bible, the Quran — each tradition has a sacred text that guides its people.
- **Abraham** — all three claim Abraham as their spiritual ancestor. They are family — a family that fights, but family.
- **The dignity of every human being** — all three traditions teach, in different ways, that human beings have inherent worth. This idea has shaped the world.

### Part 6: Where They Disagree

The disagreements are real and deep. Here are the biggest ones:

**About Jesus:** Jews do not believe Jesus was the Messiah — they are still waiting for the Messiah. Christians believe Jesus was God incarnate — God in human form. Muslims believe Jesus was a great prophet — but not God, and not the Son of God. These differences cannot be split. At least two of these positions must be wrong.

**About Muhammad:** Jews and Christians do not accept Muhammad as a prophet. Muslims believe he is the final prophet and that the Quran supersedes previous scriptures. This is a fundamental disagreement about authority.

**About how to be saved:** Judaism focuses on right living in this world — following the law, repairing the world. Christianity focuses on salvation through faith in Jesus. Islam focuses on submission to God's will and obedience to his commands. These are different answers to the question: what does God want from you?

**About each other:** The history between these three traditions includes extraordinary mutual enrichment — Islamic scholars preserving Greek philosophy, Jewish thinkers contributing to Islamic golden ages, Christianity building on Jewish scripture. It also includes extraordinary violence — the Crusades, the Inquisition, pogroms, centuries of war. The story is both, and both must be told honestly.

### Part 7: How They Worship

**Jewish worship:** The Sabbath. The synagogue. The Torah reading. The festivals — Passover (remembering the Exodus from Egypt), Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement, the most solemn day of the year), Hanukkah (the Festival of Lights). Prayer shawls, the menorah, the Star of David.

**Christian worship:** Sunday services. The Eucharist (communion). Hymns and prayers. Christmas (the birth of Jesus), Easter (the Resurrection). The cross as the central symbol. Baptism — the ritual of entering the faith.

**Islamic worship:** The five daily prayers. Friday prayers at the mosque. Ramadan and the Eid celebrations. The Hajj pilgrimage. The crescent moon. The calligraphy of the Quran as an art form — because in Islam, you do not depict God or the prophets in images; beauty is expressed through the word.

### Part 8: Why This Matters for You

You live in a world shaped by these three traditions. The laws you live under, the moral ideas in your culture, the holidays your society celebrates, the conflicts in the news — all of these have roots in the Abrahamic traditions.

Understanding these traditions from the inside — not as an outsider judging them, but as someone who genuinely grasps what each one believes and why — is essential for understanding the world. You do not have to belong to any of them. But you cannot understand the world without understanding them.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Abraham Story [Beginning of Packet]
Read the story of Abraham from an age-appropriate source. Discuss: Why is Abraham important to all three traditions? What was the covenant? What did God promise? What did Abraham promise?

### Study 2: Sacred Spaces Comparison [Early Packet]
Look at images of a synagogue, a church, and a mosque. What do they have in common? What is different? What do the differences tell you about what each tradition believes?

### Study 3: The Story of Moses and the Law [Mid-Packet]
Read the story of Moses receiving the Ten Commandments. Discuss: What are the commandments? Why do you think God gave these particular rules? Which ones do you think are most important?

### Study 4: The Story of Jesus [Mid-Packet]
Read age-appropriate accounts of Jesus's life — his birth, his teaching, his parables, his death and resurrection. Discuss: What did Jesus teach? Why did people follow him? Why was he killed? What do Christians believe happened after he died?

### Study 5: The Story of Muhammad [Mid-Packet]
Read age-appropriate accounts of Muhammad's life — the revelation, the early community, the Hijra to Medina. Discuss: What message did Muhammad receive? What are the Five Pillars? How does a Muslim's daily life reflect their faith?

### Study 6: What They Share, Where They Differ [Late Packet]
Create a three-column chart: Judaism, Christianity, Islam. Fill in: What do they believe about God? About human beings? About how to live a good life? About what happens after death? Circle what they share. Underline where they disagree.

### Study 7: The Golden Rule in Three Traditions [Late Packet]
Find each tradition's version of the Golden Rule. Compare them. Discuss: Why do all three traditions arrive at a similar moral teaching? What does this tell you about these traditions — and about human beings?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Accurate Representation
**Developing well:** The student can describe each tradition's basic beliefs in terms a real adherent would recognize as fair. They do not confuse the traditions or reduce them to stereotypes.
**Developing slowly:** The student can only describe one tradition or confuses the three. "Muslims believe in Jesus" is technically true but reveals confusion about the specific Muslim understanding.

### 2. Shared and Different
**Developing well:** The student can identify what the traditions share and where they genuinely disagree, without collapsing the differences or exaggerating them.
**Developing slowly:** Either "they're all the same" or "they all hate each other" — both are failures of nuance.

### 3. Respectful Engagement
**Developing well:** The student talks about each tradition with curiosity and respect. They do not mock unfamiliar practices or dismiss traditions they do not belong to.
**Developing slowly:** "That's weird" or "That's wrong" as the default response to unfamiliar practices.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Your Own Tradition Bias
This is the biggest risk. If you belong to one of these traditions, you will naturally present it with more depth, more warmth, and more nuance than the other two. You may not notice this. The student will. Actively compensate by investing extra preparation in the traditions that are not your own.

### The "All Basically the Same" Collapse
Some mentors, wanting to promote tolerance, suggest that the three traditions are really saying the same thing. They are not. They make incompatible claims about the nature of God, the identity of Jesus, the authority of the Quran, and the path to salvation. Presenting them as equivalent obscures the genuine disagreements that have shaped history. Respect requires accuracy, not false harmony.

### Avoiding the Violence
The student packet mentions that the history between these traditions includes violence — Crusades, Inquisition, pogroms. At Stretching Branches, you do not need to go into detail. But do not pretend the conflicts did not happen. "These traditions have sometimes fought with each other, even though they worship the same God. You will learn more about this as you get older" is sufficient and honest.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Abraham
**Strong:** Tells the story with specific detail and explains why Abraham matters to all three traditions — the covenant, the promise, the shared ancestor.
**Weak:** "He was important." No story, no specifics.

### Prompt 2: One Tradition
**Strong:** Describes the tradition from the inside — what they believe about God, how they worship, what they value. Uses specific details: the Torah, the Resurrection, the Five Pillars.
**Weak:** Generic statements that could apply to any religion.

### Prompt 6: Why They Fight
**Strong:** Shows awareness that the disagreements are real and deep — not just misunderstandings. May express genuine puzzlement about how people who worship the same God can disagree so much. This puzzlement is a sign of engagement.
**Weak:** "People are dumb" or "They just need to get along." Both avoid the question.
